title: @heynavtoor: Your AI coding agent forgets everything the moment you close the terminal. Every...
rawContent:
Tweet by @heynavtoor (Nav Toor)
Author bio: Helping you master AI daily with step-by-step AI guides, latest news, & practical tools • DM for Collabs
Text: Your AI coding agent forgets everything the moment you close the terminal. Every conversation. Every decision. Every bug fix. Gone. You re-explain your entire codebase every single morning.

Someone built a memory operating system that fixes this. Published at ACL 2026. 7,200 GitHub stars.

It's called EverOS.

Not a vector database. Not RAG with a better prompt. An actual memory layer that sits underneath your AI agent and makes it remember — across sessions, across days, across projects.

Your agent remembers what you tried last Tuesday. Remembers why it didn't work. Remembers your code style preferences without being told again. Remembers every architectural decision you made last month.

Here's how it works:

→ Three commands to install: pip install everos → everos init → everos server start
→ Dual-track memory — agent memory (cases, skills) and user memory (episodes, profile) extracted independently
→ All memory stored as Markdown files — readable, editable, grep-able, Git-versioned, openable in Obsidian
→ Local-first stack: Markdown + SQLite + LanceDB — no MongoDB, no Elasticsearch, no Redis
→ Hybrid retrieval: dense semantic vectors + sparse keyword matching + multimodal alignment
→ Multimodal ingestion — remembers text, images, audio, documents, URLs
→ Skills Evolution Engine — your agent automatically distills reusable skills from past interactions
→ 25+ runnable use cases included — from coding orchestration to Alzheimer's memory assistance to AI wearable integration

Here's what makes this technically different:

Most "memory" solutions are a vector database with a system prompt. Search your chat history. Hope the right chunk comes back. Pray the embedding captured the right context.

EverOS uses HyperMem — a hypergraph memory architecture accepted at ACL 2026. Instead of flat vector lookups, it organizes memory into topic, episode, and fact layers. One hyperedge connects multiple nodes. Cross-time associations and multi-hop reasoning built into the structure.

The result: 93%+ recall on long-term conversational tasks where standard RAG hits around 45%.

Here's the wildest part:

Their benchmark showed that adding the Skills self-evolution strategy to a 27B parameter model yielded a 234.8% increase in task success rates — matching the performance of a 397B model.

A small model with memory outperformed a model 15x its size without it.

That's the argument for memory as infrastructure, not a feature.

Works with Claude Code, Codex, OpenClaw, Hermes, and any agent that can make an API call. Apache 2.0 License.

7.2K GitHub stars. 714 forks. Research-backed. Self-hostable. Local-first.

100% Open Source.

(Link in the comments)
